sweep.rule: A fact is stated only if >=2 independent sources agree. Pending/forward events are kept in the future tense. Every datapoint is tagged sourced or estimated.
sweep.note: Refreshed on the 2-Jul-2026 (Thursday) sweep — the second trading day of Q3 and jobs day. H1 closed at records last week; the tape then softened on 1-Jul into today's data: the Dow eased 13.96pts (-0.03%) to 52,305.24, the S&P 500 slipped 0.22% to 7,483.23 and the Nasdaq Composite fell 0.66% to 26,040.03. H1 2026 still booked a strong first half (Dow +8.9%, S&P +9.6%, Nasdaq +12.8%, and the Russell 2000 ~+22% — its best first half since 1991). THE event today is the June jobs report, PULLED FORWARD to Thursday 2-Jul (8:30am ET) ahead of the Fri 3-Jul Independence-Day market holiday: consensus is ~100-115K nonfarm payrolls (FactSet median 100K, Reuters 110K) with the unemployment rate seen holding 4.3%, after May's +172K; Wednesday's ADP private payrolls came in soft at +98K (below ~110K est, down from 122K). Rates have backed UP: the 10Y sits ~4.47-4.48% (testing 4.5% intraday before easing) as the market firmed odds of a September Fed move, with Chair Warsh at the ECB's Sintra forum (1-Jul) reiterating inflation is still 'too high' while sounding open-minded on AI/productivity disinflation; the Fed's band is 3.50-3.75%. FX: DXY held ~101.3, off a ~101.6 15-month high, as the Warsh tone took the edge off; EUR/USD ~1.143 (ECB at 2.25% since 11-Jun), GBP/USD ~1.32 (BoE on hold 3.75%, next 30-Jul). The YEN remains the standout: USD/JPY printed a fresh ~40-year high 162.78 and trades ~162.67 — the weakest since 1986 — with intervention risk ACUTE into the thin-liquidity Fri 3-Jul US holiday (a classic MoF window; ~¥11.7tn already spent Apr-May). Commodities: gold RECOVERED above $4,000 to ~$4,052 (+0.5%) off this week's ~8-month low as Warsh flagged easing inflation expectations; Brent ~$74 and WTI eased to ~$67.6-68 on US-Iran de-escalation and a reopened Strait of Hormuz. VIX ~16.6. Crypto stayed weak: Bitcoin ~$58.6-60k after a ~20% June drop (its worst month of 2026) and record ~$4.5bn US spot-ETF outflows, having hit a ~21-month low ~$57.9k on 1-Jul. Politics: the USMCA/CUSMA trilateral review opened 1-Jul (16-yr extension vs terminate — Trump has threatened to end it), and the Section 122 10% tariff is set to expire 24-Jul (ruled unlawful, stayed on appeal, still collected). Earnings: Nike (FQ4, 30-Jun after close) and Micron (record FQ3, 24-Jun) are post-print reactions — Micron has since eased to ~$1,037, extending its fade off the 25-Jun ATH; Delta opens travel earnings pre-market 10-Jul. Implied moves are options-desk estimates (labelled estimated); consensus figures are flagged where not dual-confirmed.

earnings.tradeStatement: Don't chase Nike's (NKE) headline EPS beat — FQ4 diluted EPS printed $0.72 vs a ~$0.11 bar, but ~$0.52 of that is a one-off IEEPA tariff-recovery benefit (ex-item EPS ~$0.20), revenue was $11.0B (-4% currency-neutral) and FY26 landed flat at $46.4B, so the stock whipsawed and fell ~2.66% after the close; play the reaction with defined risk (cash-secured puts / reverse convertible) or wait, not a naked chase of a tariff-flattered print.
earnings.name: Nike
earnings.headline: Nike beat on a tariff-flattered EPS but fell ~2.66% after the print — the quality of the beat is the signal
earnings.thesis: Nike reported fiscal Q4 after the close on 30 June: diluted EPS $0.72 on revenue $11.0B (down 1% reported, down 4% currency-neutral), ahead of ~$10.86B expected — but the headline is flattered by a ~$0.52 benefit tied to expected recovery of IEEPA tariffs, so the underlying number is closer to ~$0.20 and the turnaround is still slow. Full-year FY26 revenue was flat at $46.4B (down 2% currency-neutral); gross profit was $5.4B and operating profit $1.3B. The market saw through it: shares whipsawed between gain and loss and settled down ~2.66% after the print. CEO Elliott Hill's 'Win Now' reset is stabilising wholesale but has not yet delivered the revenue or margin inflection that would justify multiple expansion, and the self-imposed China clear-down continues. This is a 'don't chase the headline' reaction: for books with consumer-discretionary exposure, express any constructive view with a defined-risk structure (cash-secured puts to get paid to wait, or a reverse convertible) rather than buying a tariff-flattered beat outright.
earnings.offThemeWhy: No standing desk theme on consumer discretionary — this is a single-name turnaround reaction judged on its own merit, not a strategic house view.

variant.street: Bulls seize on the big headline EPS beat and the revenue upside as proof the turnaround is landing
variant.us: Strip out the ~$0.52 tariff-recovery one-off and it's a ~$0.20 quarter on flat, currency-negative sales — better than feared, not inflecting; the ~2.66% drop confirms the market saw through it
variant.gap: The crowd anchors on the headline EPS; the edge is in the quality-of-earnings caveat and the muted price reaction

earnings.changeMyMind: Gross margins inflecting higher ex the tariff-refund and China revenue stabilising in the FY27 guide — evidence the turnaround is real, not a one-off refund, would turn the fade into an add.

pillars.note: A whipsaw that settled ~2.66% lower on a headline beat offers a modest reaction to fade, but the low bar and one-off caps the clean edge.
Street split — a big headline EPS beat against flat, currency-negative revenue and a slow turnaround; no clean alignment.
Dated, landed print (30 Jun after close) — a headline beat flattered by a tariff-recovery one-off, sold off on the day.
Beaten-down name with an eight-quarter beat streak, but no positioning extreme after the muted reaction.

earnings.result: Headline beat, soft underneath — FQ4 diluted EPS $0.72 (vs ~$0.11 est) but ~$0.52 was a one-off IEEPA tariff-recovery benefit; revenue $11.0B (down 1% reported / 4% currency-neutral) vs ~$10.86B est; gross profit $5.4B, operating profit $1.3B; FY26 revenue flat at $46.4B (down 2% currency-neutral)
earnings.reaction: Shares whipsawed between gain and loss and fell ~2.66% after the close as the market discounted the tariff-flattered headline EPS
earnings.watch: Gross-margin recovery ex the tariff-refund one-off, the China revenue trajectory, wholesale re-stocking and the FY27 guide.

facts.text: Nike reported FQ4 FY26 after the close on 30 Jun: diluted EPS $0.72 (vs ~$0.11 est) on revenue $11.0B (down 1% reported, 4% currency-neutral), beating ~$10.86B; ~$0.52 of the EPS was a one-off IEEPA tariff-recovery benefit
Full-year FY26 revenue was flat at $46.4B (down 2% currency-neutral); gross profit $5.4B, operating profit $1.3B; shares fell ~2.66% after the close, whipsawing between gain and loss

earnings.tradeStatement: Stay long Micron (MU) but express it with discipline — a week-plus after the record FQ3 (rev $41.46B, EPS $25.11, ~$50B Q4 guide, GM ~86%) the stock has kept fading, easing to ~$1,037 (~15% off the 25-Jun ATH near $1,213) as the post-print momentum rolled over into a softer, rate-wary tape; the HBM/AI-memory thesis is confirmed AND priced, so for concentrated holders monetise the level with a zero-cost collar or call-overwrite, and add on the pullbacks the fade is now handing you rather than chasing.
earnings.name: Micron Technology
earnings.headline: Micron's record blow-out is confirmed and priced — and the fade has extended to ~$1,037, ~15% off its ATH.
earnings.thesis: Micron reported FQ3 (ended 28-May) after the close on 24 June and it was the print of the quarter: record revenue $41.46B (beating ~$35.25B consensus), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28), and a Q4 guide of ~$50B ±$1B (~86% gross margin). HBM3E/HBM4 are sold out through calendar 2027 with demand into 2028; HBM4 12-high is ramping ~2x faster than HBM3E. The stock gapped to an all-time high near $1,213 on 25 June (Goldman lifted its target to $1,100), then FADED — trading ~$1,037 by 1-2 July, ~15% off the peak, as the post-print momentum rolled over and the broad tape softened into jobs day and a 10Y backed up toward 4.5%. The 2027 pricing thesis is confirmed but largely in the price — this is a 'how do I hold a name that just made and gave back a new high' problem, not a 'buy the event'. For concentrated holders, monetise the move with a collar or overwrite; for others, the post-ATH fade is now handing you the dip to add into, not the strength.

variant.street: Analysts raised targets across the board on the record print and ~$50B guide (Goldman to $1,100)
variant.us: Thesis confirmed but largely priced after a gap to ATHs, now fading ~15% off the peak — hold via a collar/overwrite, add on the dips the fade is handing you
variant.gap: The crowd chased the gap; the edge now is in the expression (monetise the move) and buying the post-ATH fade rather than the strength

earnings.changeMyMind: Guidance or channel checks pointing to 2027 HBM pricing rolling over, or the booked-capacity contracts being renegotiated — the 2027 pricing path, not the quarter just reported, is the thesis.

pillars.note: A record blow-out gapped to ATHs and has since faded ~15% — limited clean edge chasing; the retracement is now offering a better add level than the strength did.
Unanimously bullish sell-side; Goldman raised its target to $1,100 from $900 and the cohort lifted estimates on the ~$50B guide.
Dated, landed blow-out (24 Jun) — record revenue and a guide-up that re-rated the name.
Crowded long that gapped to ATHs then gave ~15% back — momentum has clearly rolled over, not a washed-out or short-squeeze setup.

earnings.result: Blow-out beat — record revenue $41.46B (vs ~$35.25B est), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28); Q4 guided to ~$50B ±$1B with gross margin ~86%; HBM sold out through CY2027 into 2028
earnings.reaction: Shares gapped to an all-time high near $1,213 on 25 Jun (Goldman raised its target to $1,100 from $900), then faded ~15% to ~$1,037 by 1-2 Jul as momentum rolled over into a softer, rate-wary tape
earnings.watch: 2027 HBM pricing durability, the HBM4 ramp, and whether the post-ATH fade finds support or extends with the AI/semis complex off its highs.

facts.text: Micron reported FQ3 FY26 (ended 28-May) on 24 Jun: record revenue $41.46B (beat ~$35.25B), non-GAAP EPS $25.11 (vs ~$20.28)
Q4 guided to ~$50B ±$1B with gross margin ~86%; HBM3E/HBM4 booked through CY2027, demand into 2028
MU gapped to an ATH near $1,213 on 25 Jun, then faded ~15% to ~$1,037 by 1-2 Jul as the broad tape softened into jobs day

earnings.tradeStatement: Long Delta Air Lines (DAL) into Q2 before the open on Friday 10 July — a bellwether read on summer-travel demand and premium-cabin resilience into a lowered bar (Street sees EPS ~$1.43, down ~32% YoY) with cheaper jet fuel (WTI now ~$67-68) a margin tailwind; express it with a defined-risk structure (cash-secured puts / reverse convertible) given the low bar, not naked stock.
earnings.name: Delta Air Lines
earnings.headline: Delta kicks off travel earnings pre-market 10 July — a lowered bar into peak summer, with cheaper jet fuel a tailwind
earnings.thesis: Delta reports fiscal Q2 2026 before the opening bell on Friday 10 July — the first read on how peak-summer travel demand and premium-cabin/loyalty resilience are holding up. Consensus sits around $1.43 diluted EPS (some models ~$1.48), a steep ~31.9% drop from $2.10 a year ago, so the bar is deliberately low. The setup has two supports: softer jet fuel — WTI has fallen further to ~$67-68 (Brent ~$74) as the US-Iran war premium bled out and the Strait of Hormuz reopened — is a direct margin tailwind, and Delta's mix has shifted toward higher-margin premium and loyalty revenue (management guides low-teens Q2 revenue growth on flat capacity) that is less sensitive to a slowing main cabin. This is off the desk's strategic themes but a genuine dated catalyst for any book with consumer-cyclical or travel exposure, and a bellwether for the broader industrials/consumer read into Q3. Best expressed pre-print with a defined-risk structure — cash-secured puts to get paid to wait for a better entry, or a reverse convertible — rather than naked stock into an event.
earnings.offThemeWhy: No standing desk theme on airlines/travel — this is a single-name, dated earnings catalyst judged on its own merit, not a strategic house view.
earnings.changeMyMind: A guide-down on softening main-cabin demand or a corporate-travel air-pocket outweighing the fuel tailwind — evidence the consumer is rolling over turns the pre-position into a pass.

pillars.note: A low bar (EPS down ~32% YoY) into peak season caps the asymmetry, but the cheaper-fuel tailwind and premium mix skew the risk toward 'better than feared'.
Street cautious — EPS guided sharply lower YoY; no clean buy-side alignment ahead of the print.
Dated Q2 print before the open on 10 Jul — a real, scheduled single-name catalyst that also opens travel-earnings season.
Cyclical, sentiment-sensitive name; some caution priced but no clean positioning extreme into the print.

earnings.watch: Summer-travel demand and load factors, premium-cabin/loyalty revenue, unit costs (CASM) against the cheaper jet-fuel tailwind, and the full-year guide.

facts.text: Delta reports fiscal Q2 2026 before the opening bell on Friday 10 July; consensus diluted EPS ~$1.43 (some models ~$1.48), down ~31.9% from $2.10 a year ago; management guides low-teens Q2 revenue growth on flat capacity
Jet-fuel input costs have eased further as WTI fell to ~$67-68 and Brent to ~$74 after the US-Iran war premium bled out and the Strait of Hormuz reopened — a direct margin tailwind into the print

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long gold (XAU) — accumulate ~$4,050 as strategic ballast; a bet on continued central-bank buying and currency debasement, with bullion just bouncing back above $4,000 off an ~8-month low as Warsh flagged easing inflation expectations — sized to the protection gap, not a tactical momentum trade.
exEarnings.name: Gold — accumulate the pullback
exEarnings.headline: Gold bounces back above $4,000 to ~$4,052 off an ~8-month low — the accumulation window is still open
exEarnings.thesis: Gold slipped to an ~8-month low near $4,000 earlier this week — roughly 27% below its January record — as strong US data and a firm dollar absorbed safe-haven flows, then RECOVERED above $4,000 to ~$4,052 (+0.5%) after Chair Warsh flagged that inflation expectations had eased over the past month. The strategic case is unchanged: central banks stay net buyers and every major bank's year-end target sits well above spot. For under-protected books this dip-and-bounce is still the accumulation window in the cleanest debasement-and-geopolitics hedge — sized to the protection gap, bought into weakness rather than chased, with a hawkish-hold Fed and a firm dollar the near-term headwind and the marginal official buyers the underpin.

variant.street: Every major bank bullish — year-end targets well above spot; central banks net buyers
variant.us: Same direction — accumulate into under-protected books on the dip; the bounce off the ~8-month low shows the debasement bid is still there

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Central banks turning net sellers for two consecutive quarters, or real yields breaking decisively higher.

pillars.note: Bank targets ~30-50% above spot versus gold's normal trailing move — roughly a 1.5-2x asymmetry to the upside from ~$4,050.
No single dated catalyst — a structural accumulation; near-term the firm dollar and hawkish-hold Fed are the headwind, softened by Warsh's easing-expectations remark.
Every major bank's year-end target sits above spot and central banks are net buyers — sell-side and flow both confirm.
Driver specificity yes (sourced central-bank tonnage and named bank targets), but no variant edge — this is the consensus call, not a differentiated one.
Core to the Gold & Currency house view as strategic ballast.
~27% off the highs with positioning reset, now bouncing off an 8-month low — neither washed-out nor crowded; mild fuel.
Reclaimed $4,000 to ~$4,052 after an ~8-month low; the long-term uptrend (above the rising 200-day) is intact but the near-term trend is only stabilising — mixed.

macro.detail: ~27% below the 28-Jan record of $5,589; recovered above $4,000 to ~$4,052 (+0.5%) off this week's ~8-month low after Warsh flagged easing inflation expectations. Central banks net +244t in Q1 and +17t in April; China adding for 18 straight months.
macro.watch: The firm dollar / hawkish-hold Fed as the near-term headwind; central-bank demand as the marginal buyer underpinning the dip; today's June payrolls as the next dollar test.

facts.text: Gold recovered above $4,000 to ~$4,052 (+0.5%) around 2 Jul, off an ~8-month low near $4,000 hit earlier this week (about 27% below the 28-Jan record of $5,589), after Warsh flagged easing inflation expectations
Gold had been on track for a fourth straight monthly loss as markets firmed odds of a 2026 Fed hike, before the mid-week bounce
Central banks net buyers: +244t in Q1 2026, +17t in April; China 18 consecutive months; bank year-end targets Goldman $5,400, JPMorgan ~$6,000, Morgan Stanley $5,200, UBS $5,500

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long the power & grid build-out (utilities, gas, nuclear) — a lower-beta way to be long the AI theme that pays contracted income; a bet on structural datacenter electricity demand, not on interest rates — and a steadier place to sit than the chip complex, which is off its highs as the tape wobbles into jobs day.
exEarnings.name: AI power & the grid
exEarnings.headline: Electricity is the binding constraint on AI — and the lower-beta way to own it
exEarnings.thesis: Datacenter electricity demand is on track for roughly 1,050 TWh in 2026 — doubling in a few years — with gas and nuclear the dispatchable supply. Owning the power and grid build-out is the lower-beta way to stay long the AI theme, and it pays contracted income while it compounds. The AI chip complex round-tripped a full drawdown into a record H1 close and has since eased off its highs (Nasdaq -0.66% on 1-Jul, MU fading ~15% off its ATH) as the tape turns rate-wary into jobs day — and that volatility is the point: the power bottleneck behind the build-out (with Micron's record HBM print a reminder the demand keeps scaling) is a steadier expression of the same secular story.

variant.street: Crowds the AI chip names to own the theme
variant.us: Own the power and grid bottleneck instead — lower-beta, contracted income, still under-owned, calmer when chips whipsaw
variant.gap: The market pays up for compute and underprices the electricity constraint behind it

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Datacenter load-growth forecasts being cut, or utility rate-base and PPA approvals stalling.

pillars.note: Datacenter power demand roughly doubling — a large structural move versus the sector's normal drift.
A structural demand story rather than a single dated event.
IEA data and some sell-side support, but no independent breakout / flow confirmation yet.
Quantified driver (IEA: datacenter load ~1,050 TWh, roughly doubling) and a real variant — own the power bottleneck rather than the crowded, more-volatile chips. Driver yes, variant yes.
Core Power & Infrastructure house view.
Still under-owned versus the chip names — the crowd is clearly not yet with us, which is fuel.
Multi-year rate-base and load growth; the trend is constructive but there's no clean breakout off the moving averages yet — mixed.

macro.detail: IEA projects datacenter electricity use approaching ~1,050 TWh by 2026; dispatchable gas and nuclear lead the supply, with SMRs entering after 2030. A lower-beta sleeve while the AI chip complex sits off its highs and the tape turns rate-wary.
macro.watch: Utility rate-base approvals, datacenter PPAs and nuclear/SMR newsflow.

facts.text: IEA: datacenter electricity use approaching ~1,050 TWh by 2026, more than double recent levels
Gas and nuclear are the dispatchable supply; SMRs enter the mix after 2030

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Long high-quality duration — extend from cash / bills into 5-10y govt / IG to lock ~4.5% yields; a bet that carry plus eventual cuts beats cash, accepting mark-to-market risk while the Fed stays hawkish — with the 10Y having backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (near 4.5%) into today's pulled-forward June payrolls, a better entry than a week ago.
exEarnings.name: Quality carry — get paid to wait
exEarnings.headline: The 10Y has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% into jobs day — own the carry at a better level
exEarnings.thesis: May PCE (25 June) printed broadly in line at 4.1% headline with core 3.4% — still the hottest core in over two years — and the Fed held at 3.50-3.75% on 17 June with a hawkish dot plot; the market has since FIRMED odds of a September move, and the 10Y has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (testing 4.5% intraday before easing on Warsh's easing-expectations remark). The 'extend before the cuts' convexity trade is on ice, but the case left is simpler and now better priced — you are paid ~4.5% in high-quality intermediate bonds to wait, ballast that cash can't give a book that carried equities to a record H1. Today's pulled-forward June payrolls (consensus ~100-115K, u/e 4.3%; ADP soft at +98K) is the next test of the range — a soft print would reward the extra yield you're locking. Add for income and diversification, not for a quick gain.

variant.street: The Street is split between a 2026 hike and a 2027 pivot; the back-up to ~4.5% reflects the hawkish lean
variant.us: Own quality carry now at ~4.5% — paid ballast that rallies in a risk-off; don't bank on near-term cuts (or fear every hike)
variant.gap: The market may still be too optimistic on the timing of the first cut, but the back-up has improved the entry

exEarnings.changeMyMind: Inflation rolling back toward target (pulling the first cut forward), or the Fed actually hiking through 4.5% — either end of the barbell breaks the simple carry case.

pillars.note: Earning ~4.5% with cuts a 2027 story — a carry trade with limited near-term convexity, but the back-up to ~4.47-4.48% has improved the entry versus a week ago.
The 25-Jun PCE landed in line and the 17-Jun Fed held hawkish; today's pulled-forward June payrolls is the next dated test — structural, not a single imminent trigger.
The PCE print and the 10Y back-up are sourced fact, but the Street is split on the path — alignment is weaker than a clean carry consensus.
Quantified, sourced drivers (PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%, hawkish hold, 10Y ~4.47-4.48%), but mostly the consensus read — a modest variant on cut timing.
Core Fixed Income house view — quality income and ballast, even with the cut delayed.
The crowd is still parked in cash and bills; extending is the contrarian-leaning move.
10Y backing up to ~4.47-4.48% toward the 4.5% test; the price trend is soft — a better entry for a carry buyer, mixed for a mark-to-market holder.

macro.detail: May PCE (25 Jun) printed in line at 4.1% headline, core 3.4%. The Fed held 3.50-3.75% on 17 Jun with a hawkish dot plot; the market has firmed September-move odds and the 10Y has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (near 4.5%) into today's pulled-forward June payrolls (consensus ~100-115K, u/e 4.3%; ADP +98K).
macro.watch: Today's June payrolls, then the late-July FOMC — whether the data hardens the hawkish path; and whether duration holds its diversification value as equities sit near records.

facts.text: May PCE printed broadly in line at 4.1% y/y headline with core 3.4% (released 25 Jun); the Fed held 3.50-3.75% on 17 Jun with a hawkish dot plot

The 10Y Treasury yield has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (testing 4.5% intraday) as markets firmed odds of a September Fed move; Warsh (Sintra, 1-Jul) said inflation is still 'too high' while flagging easing expectations
The June jobs report was pulled forward to Thu 2-Jul (8:30am ET) ahead of the Fri 3-Jul holiday — consensus ~100-115K, u/e 4.3%; ADP private payrolls +98K

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Buy a ~12-month 10-year Treasury range accrual note — earn an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the 10Y yield fixes inside a 4.00-4.90% band; with the 10Y at ~4.47-4.48% (backed up toward the top of the band but still under the proven 5% ceiling) you are paid to hold the view that a hawkish-hold Fed keeps the long end boxed, forgoing the coupon only on a decisive break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
exEarnings.name: 10Y range accrual
exEarnings.trigger: The 25-Jun in-line PCE (4.1% headline, core 3.4%) and the 17-Jun hawkish Fed hold cap the 10Y from both sides — a sticky-inflation floor that stops it rallying under 4%, a policy path anchored against a runaway break through 5%. The 10Y has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (testing 4.5%), sitting in the upper half of the band but still comfortably below the 5% ceiling, into today's pulled-forward June payrolls and the late-July FOMC — exactly the boxed-in regime a range accrual is paid to harvest.
exEarnings.headline: The in-line PCE and hawkish hold box the 10Y below 5% — get paid an enhanced coupon to hold the range
exEarnings.thesis: May PCE printed in line at 4.1% (core 3.4%) on 25 June and the Fed held hawkish on 17 June — a stance that boxes in the 10Y from both sides, with the 10Y now at ~4.47-4.48% after backing up toward the top of the band into jobs day. The TOP of the band is the harder call and the case is specific: 5% has been a proven ceiling — the 10Y peaked near 5.0% in October 2023 and has failed there on every test since, because at ~5% nominal (~2%+ real) duration is the cheapest in two decades and each approach draws heavy real-money buying — pensions and insurers matching liabilities, foreign reserve managers, and asset allocators rotating out of richly-valued equities. The BOTTOM (~4.00%) is held by sticky inflation — PCE at 4.1% — plus no cuts until 2027 to fuel a rally and an elevated term premium on heavy Treasury supply. So a 12-month 10Y range accrual pays an enhanced ~7.5% p.a. coupon (vs ~4.0% on cash) for each day the yield fixes inside 4.00-4.90%: you are paid to hold the boxed-in view, with the coupon forgone only on the days it breaks out. Capital is returned at par at maturity (issuer credit aside) — more than cash without an outright duration bet.

variant.street: Bears see fiscal/supply pushing the 10Y toward 5%; bulls wait for cuts to pull it under 4% — both expect a break
variant.us: Neither break holds: 5% is a proven buyer's ceiling and ~4% a sticky-inflation floor — monetise the range rather than bet on the break
variant.gap: The market pays up for the tail break in either direction and under-prices the 10Y simply staying boxed around 4.4-4.5%

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A decisive break of the box — a growth scare or sharp disinflation that pulls the 10Y below 4.00% (cuts brought forward), or a fiscal/supply/inflation shock that drives it through 5.00% — either zeros the accrual.

pillars.note: An enhanced coupon (~7.5% vs ~4% cash) for the 10Y simply staying boxed in — a steady carry pickup with capped, income-style upside rather than an outright duration bet.
Dated, landed catalysts — the 25-Jun in-line PCE and 17-Jun hawkish Fed hold that cap the 10Y from both ends, with today's June payrolls the next range test.
The higher-for-longer, range-bound-10Y read is broadly shared, but the precise 4.00-4.90% band and the coupon edge are an estimated structuring call.

Sourced drivers (PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%, hawkish hold, cuts to 2027, 10Y ~4.47-4.48%) plus a specific two-sided case: a proven 5% buyer's ceiling above and a sticky-PCE / supply term-premium floor below.
Core Fixed Income / income house view — paid carry from a boxed-in long end, no outright duration call.
The crowd is parked in cash and bills or positioned for a 10Y break either way; selling 10Y rate-range optionality for an enhanced coupon is the contrarian income move.
The 10Y at ~4.47-4.48% in the upper half of the band, repeatedly rejected at 5% and bid toward 4% — a well-defined range, the accrual's friend.

macro.metric: PCE 4.1% / core 3.4%; hawkish hold; 10Y ~4.47-4.48%, capped at 5%
macro.detail: 12-month 10Y Treasury range accrual: ~4.00-4.90% band, ~7.5% p.a. enhanced coupon vs ~4.0% on cash, A-rated issuer, capital at par. Coupon accrues daily for each in-range fixing; forgone on a break above 4.90% or below 4.00%.
macro.watch: Today's June payrolls, the late-July FOMC and Treasury supply/auctions — a shock that drives the 10Y through 5% or under 4% and zeros the accrual.

facts.text: May PCE printed in line at 4.1% headline / core 3.4% (25 Jun); the Fed held hawkish on 17 Jun, and the market has firmed odds of a September move
The 10Y Treasury yield has backed up to ~4.47-4.48% (testing 4.5%); it last peaked near 5.0% in Oct 2023 and has failed at that level on every test since
Sticky PCE at 4.1% and heavy Treasury supply keep an inflation/term premium in long yields, a floor near 4%

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Reduce / hedge USD exposure in EUR- and GBP-base books — effectively short the dollar against the home currency — a bet that the post-peak USD weakens into 2026 as the Fed-ECB spread eventually narrows; this is NOT a call on any single pair's spot level, nor specifically a yen- or euro-strength view.
exEarnings.name: Hedge the USD concentration
exEarnings.headline: Dollar holding ~101.3 off a 15-month high — a prime window to hedge USD-heavy non-dollar books on jobs day
exEarnings.thesis: The dollar pushed to a 15-month high (DXY ~101.6) on the hawkish Fed and firmer September-hike odds before easing to ~101.3 as Warsh flagged softer inflation expectations at Sintra; EUR/USD sits ~1.143 and cable ~1.32 — even as every 2026 bank outlook still calls a post-peak dollar and an eventually-narrowing Fed-ECB spread. For EUR- and GBP-base books carrying outsized USD sleeves, this stretched level — the dollar pausing near the highs with today's pulled-forward June payrolls a catalyst that could crack it either way — is the window to hedge the currency mismatch: currency-hedged share classes for Retail, FX forwards or zero-cost collars for Professionals — sized to the mismatch, not run as a directional bet.

variant.street: Major-bank consensus is a post-peak dollar — more USD weakness into 2026 as the Fed-ECB rate spread narrows
variant.us: Same direction; use the hawkish-driven dollar strength (and the pause near the highs) to put hedges on USD-heavy non-dollar books at a better level
variant.gap: The near-term firm dollar is a hedging gift, not a reason to stay unhedged

exEarnings.changeMyMind: The Fed out-hiking every other central bank on a sustained inflation overshoot — a structurally higher-for-longer dollar would delay the turn the hedge is sized for.

pillars.note: A hedge caps a tail (an adverse FX move on a large USD sleeve) rather than chasing a big gain — modest, defined asymmetry.
Dated and live: the dollar's push to a 15-month high now pausing ~101.3 into today's pulled-forward June payrolls — a concrete window to hedge into.
Bank 2026 outlooks broadly agree on a post-peak dollar and a narrowing Fed-ECB spread; the firm spot is the confirming signal.
Specific driver (a book's non-base USD %) and a clear variant (hedge into the firmness), but the entry timing is a soft, estimated call.
Direct expression of the Gold & Currency house view — currency diversification for concentrated-USD books.
Dollar longs are crowded after the hawkish repricing — hedging when the crowd is max-long USD is reasonable timing, not an extreme.
DXY off a 15-month high to ~101.3 — mixed; a level to fade rather than chase.

macro.detail: The dollar pushed to a 15-month high (~101.6) on the hawkish Fed and firmer September-hike odds before easing to ~101.3 as Warsh flagged softer inflation expectations, ahead of today's pulled-forward June payrolls (EUR/USD ~1.143, GBP/USD ~1.32); 2026 bank outlooks still describe a post-peak dollar with the Fed-ECB spread set to narrow. USD/JPY has pushed to a fresh ~40-year high 162.78 on the wide differential.

exEarnings.tradeStatement: Earn the carry on the wide US rate differential — effectively long the high-yielding USD versus a low-yielder (JPY / EUR) via dual-currency deposits — a bet that the differential persists and the pair stays range-bound so you collect the coupon; it is NOT a directional spot view, and the worst case is converting into the currency you'd have bought anyway.
exEarnings.name: FX carry & dual-currency income
exEarnings.headline: Monetise the wide rate differential — FX carry and dual-currency income, but mind the intervention line into the holiday
exEarnings.thesis: With the front end held high by the hawkish Fed and rate differentials wide, income books can be paid to express a range view on pairs they are happy to hold either side of. Dual-currency deposits and currency-hedged carry turn the differential into an enhanced coupon — you accept conversion at a set rate in return for yield. A Professional, range-bound income tool, not a directional dollar bet — and with USD/JPY at a fresh ~40-year high (162.78, ~162.67 now), through the 162.00 level several banks call the intervention 'line in the sand', with intervention risk ACUTE into the thin-liquidity Fri 3-Jul US holiday and the BoJ next meeting 31-Jul, size the conversion strikes with real care.

variant.street: Differentials favour the dollar now; a yen and euro recovery is a 2026 story as the gap narrows
variant.us: Earn the carry while the differential is wide, on pairs you would hold either side of
variant.gap: Get paid to wait for the FX moves the house expects later

exEarnings.changeMyMind: A fast, disorderly dollar reversal — e.g. a large MoF intervention or an abrupt dovish Fed pivot — that compresses the differential and collapses the carry.

pillars.note: Carry is steady coupon income with a defined conversion risk — low, bounded asymmetry by design.
Soft and ongoing: the hawkish hold keeps the front end and the differential wide, but there is no single dated trigger.
The wide US rate differential is a hard, confirmable macro fact and the income angle sits squarely in the house view.
Specific driver (the rate gap) and a clear variant (get paid to wait), but the entry and pair selection are soft, estimated calls.
On the Gold & Currency house view — currency diversification and income from the rate differential.
Carry trades are well-populated and crowded at the highs — not at a clear unwind extreme, but acute intervention risk into the holiday argues for care.
USD/JPY trends with the differential at ~40-year highs, through the 162 intervention line — constructive for carry but stretched into intervention territory; mixed.

exEarnings.name: USD/JPY downside
exEarnings.trigger: Still invalid on the 2-Jul tape: a one-way yen-strength bet has no live justification while the carry is winning and spot is making highs. USD/JPY printed a fresh ~40-year high 162.78 and trades ~162.67 — through the 162.00 intervention 'line in the sand' — with NO confirmed intervention-driven reversal yet. Acute intervention risk into the thin Fri 3-Jul holiday raises the odds of a snap, but until MoF actually stalls and reverses spot (a confirmed lower high) this is a knife-catch. The two-sided strangle is the right way to own the intervention binary here, not directional puts. Held back.

exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY through 162 at ~40-year highs — directional yen-strength puts have no live trigger; own the two-sided strangle instead
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY sits at ~40-year highs — a fresh 162.78 high, ~162.67 now, through the 162.00 level several banks call the intervention 'line in the sand', deep in MoF/BoJ territory, held up by a wide US-Japan differential after the in-line PCE and hawkish Fed. A one-way short-dated put trade is a fast yen-strength snapback bet — but with spot still making highs and the carry dominant, there is no live justification to own directional downside here. This trade only re-arms if intervention actually stalls and reverses spot (a confirmed lower high). Until then it is held back in favour of the two-sided strangle — even with intervention risk acute into the thin holiday window.

exEarnings.headline: ECB at 2.25% but the dollar is firm — a EUR/USD call spread that needs a dollar turn to arm
exEarnings.thesis: The ECB raised its policy rate to 2.25% on 11 June while the Fed's hawkish hold keeps it on pause — the Fed-ECB spread is set to narrow eventually, the core of the post-peak-dollar / euro-recovery view. But near-term the in-line PCE and a dollar holding ~101.3 (off a 15-month high) have EUR/USD pinned ~1.143. A 1.18/1.21 call spread is the cheap, capped way to own the eventual catch-up — but it only arms once EUR/USD reclaims ~1.17 on a softening dollar, the catalyst for which could be a soft US jobs print today (ADP already soft at +98K). Until then, held back.

With cable near 7-month lows and the 1.31 short strike close to spot, the assignment risk is live and the upside trigger is far off. Held back until the dollar turns and cable reclaims 1.355.

Entry ~162.7, take profit on a break beyond 160 or 166, let it decay / cut if pinned 161-164 (theta).
exEarnings.name: USD/JPY event volatility
exEarnings.trigger: Live and sharper on the 2-Jul tape: USD/JPY printed a fresh ~40-year high 162.78 and trades ~162.67 — the weakest yen since 1986 — through the 162.00 level several banks call the intervention 'line in the sand'. Intervention risk is ACUTE into the thin-liquidity Fri 3-Jul US holiday (a classic MoF window — low liquidity magnifies the effect; ~¥11.7tn already spent Apr-May), with the BoJ hike-biased into its 31-Jul meeting, the carry still dominant, AND today's pulled-forward June payrolls (8:30am ET) as a fresh, immediate vol catalyst. The next move is a genuinely two-sided binary — a payrolls/carry-driven melt-up vs a holiday-window intervention snapback. Owning the move either way has a live, and today heightened, justification.
exEarnings.headline: USD/JPY ~162.7 through the intervention line with acute MoF risk into a thin holiday — own the two-sided move with a short-dated strangle
exEarnings.thesis: USD/JPY printed a fresh ~40-year high 162.78 and trades ~162.67 — the weakest yen since 1986 and through the 162.00 level several banks now call the intervention 'line in the sand' — with officials having already spent ~¥11.7tn in Apr-May, 'when, not if' the watchword, the wide differential keeping the carry dominant and the BoJ hike-biased into its 31-Jul meeting. The next move is genuinely two-sided and the catalysts cluster THIS window: today's pulled-forward June payrolls (8:30am ET) is an immediate vol trigger, and the thin-liquidity Fri 3-Jul US holiday is a classic MoF intervention window — fresh intervention could snap spot sharply lower, while if the carry keeps winning it squeezes higher through 164. When you are confident in a big move but not its sign, own the volatility — a 2-4wk strangle (~160 put / ~166 call) profits on a break beyond either wing, with the two premiums as the defined max loss. Take profit on a break beyond 160 or 166; let it decay/expire if spot stays pinned 161-164.
